messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.3 Summary Specific Needs Expected Results D & E & C Measures • Current SAF supply is low at <0.05% of total EU aviation fuel use. To meet sustainability targets, our SAF supply must be increased (~2.3 Million Tonnes by 2030, 28.6 MT by 2050). • SAFs are currently more expensive than fossil-based jet fuel. • Algae biofuels are currently not sustainable and cost-competitive due to a range of factors: - High biomass production cost (& limited feedstock availability) due to lack of access to cheap and abundant supply of nutrients. - Culture crash due to bacterial contamination - High biorefinery cost due to high energy requirements associated with cell disruption and high solvent requirements. - Low fuel conversion efficiency due to limited understanding of the thermochemical routes for conversion of biomass components to SAFs and the required purification steps. A more sustainable, cost-competitive and efficient process for the production of algae SAFs via technologies that enable production of microalgae on cheap and abundant CO2 and waste nutrients from the AD sector: - Autotrophic growth strategy on digestate - Membrane system for CO2 delivery to microalgae culture - In-line sensor for bacterial detection/enumeration - UV irradiation system for bacterial control - Low-energy cell disruption based on autolysis - Lipid extraction strategy to minimise solvent usage - Multifunctional catalysts with H2 looping route for conversion of algal lipids to HEFA-SPK - Algae-specific HEFA-SPK purification method - Route for the conversion of microalgae biomass residue to TCR-HDO oil (kerosene mixture) - Algae-specific TCR-HDO purification method - Pilot plant for full-system process (TRL4 /5) of converting digestate to microalgae-based aviation fuel - Process simulations, business-modelling, life-cycle analysis and policy assessment/recommendation. Dissemination measures: 20+ Industry Workshops, networking events, webinars, and seminars; Workshops at pilot-scale algae fuel facility on AD site; 10+ open-access publications; >12 presentations and >2 conference workshops; Microalgae Fuel Summer Research School; Technical brochures; Policy white paper; SusAlgaeFuel Final Conference. Exploitation measures: ■ IP protection for new technologies followed by open-access publications and open-science repository according to Data Management Plan; ■ BMCs and Business/commercialisation plans; ■ Policy white paper; ■ RSB certification Communication measures: SusAlgaeFuel public website and social-media channels; Communication training programme; Promotional contents (infographics, leaflets, posters, project videos), Journalistic content (e.g. interviews, press release); Women-in-Science campaign. Target Groups Outcomes Impacts - AD operators as technology adopters - Algae producers as technology providers - Fuel/SAF producers - Aviation sector: Aircraft manufacturers; Leasing facilities; Airports & airlines - By-product users: Biofertiliser & feed producers - Sensor manufacturers - Fuel certification authorities - Industry associations - Academic community / researchers - EU projects in related area - Policymakers and MPs (national & EU^) - Investors in green technology - University students (undergraduate, master, PhD) - General public/air passengers - Further innovative/disruptive research in microalgae and SAF production on the basis of our findings, leading to IP generation, licenses, and spin-outs. - Commercialisation plan for post-project scale up of our SusAlgaeFuel process & adoption by SAF producers, AD operators & businesses along aviation fuel supply chain - Robust mechanisms and research to increase the scale and reduce the cost of algal biofuels to <35 €/MWh by 2030 according to SET plan 8 mandate, increasing the efficiency, and decreasing GHG emissions of our algae SAFs. - Positive policy changes at national and EU levels in relation to SAF adoption, phasing out of fossil fuels and creating subsidies/incentives for SAF commercialization - Greater awareness of the importance of SAF amongst the general public, with an effective focus on air passengers - Scientific and technological: Groundbreaking innovative approaches that work in tandem to enable a more cost-competitive, efficient, and circular production of microalgae SAFs, complemented with process simulations, techno-economic and LCA to assess scalability and identify process improvements. - Energy security in the EU and economic: Delivering world’s 1st microalgae HEFA-SPK and TCR-HDO fuels tested for compliance to ASTM jet-fuel specifications as microalgae substitute aviation fuel with potential to annually treat 172 Mt of digestate for the production of 1 Mt of HEFA-SPK (equivalent to 20% of Europe’s projected SAF requirements of 5 Mt in 2030). [HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels] 30 3 QUALITY AND EFFICIENCY OF IMPLEMENTATION 3.1. Work Plan and Resources Our SusAlgaeFuel project is divided into eight interconnected WPs which work to complement each other in delivering an integrated technology, business model, sustainability and policy solution for algal aviation fuel development in the EU. The interconnectivity between the WPs is illustrated in Figure 3.1a. WPs 1-4 will provide core technology development at bench scale, while WP5 will use the information obtained in these WPs to assess commercial and socio-environmental scalability of the system. Each WP is further divided into several Tasks to address specific objectives. In WP6, the processes developed in WPs 1-4, complemented with process optimisation based on WP5, will be transferred to pilot scale to demonstrate scalability, culminating in the production of sufficient SAF for ASTM compliance test at the end of our project lifetime. We will adopt a staggered approach, with WP6 starting only in Month 25 (2nd phase of our project), allowing investigation in WPs 1-4 to reach sufficient maturity prior to scale-up. WP7 provides blueprint for results dissemination, commercialisation and policy guidance. Figure 3.1a: Our SusAlgaeFuel approach and interconnectivity between WPs Management Structure: The Executive Board (EB) assumes the overarching leadership for our project. It decides on the general policy and strategic orientations of our project to ensure that milestones and deliverables are achieved according to the timeline. EB is responsible for monitoring the overall progress of our project and designing corrective actions or authorising the execution of risk mitigation strategies when significant project delay/deviation is observed. The EB comprises all WP leaders and our project coordinator. The experienced Project Coordinator (UCD) has the overall responsibility to manage and execute our project. They also act as an intermediary between the European Commission, Advisory Board (which currently consists of Boeing and CityJet), EB and the rest of our consortium. WP Leaders are in charge of: (a) implementing decisions taken by the EB for their WPs, (b) coordinating the activities within their WPs and monitoring progress, (c) reporting to our Project Coordinator, and (d) organizing and chairing online WP meetings. The Project Office (PO) at UCD will manage the daily logistics of coordination activities on behalf of our Project Coordinator, including the organisation of on-site consortium meetings at M1, M18, M36 and M48 Table 3.1a: List of Work Packages WP No. WP Title Lead Part.No Lead Partner Person-Months Start Month End Month 1 High-density cultivation through biogenic CO2 capture and waste utilisation 1 UCD 39.5 1 36 2 Sensor system for bacterial detection and culture purification 3 IRIS 42.0 1 36 3 Cascading biorefinery for biomass purification 5 KIT 63.0 1 36 4 High-efficiency fuel conversion and purification 6 UCAM 70.7 1 36 5 Process integration and whole life-cycle analysis 8 SFS 38.0 13 48 6 Pilot scale-up, fuel testing and digital twin 3 A4F 154.0 25 48 7 Communication, policy and exploitation roadmap 9 ESCI 46.7 1 48 8 Project coordination 1 UCD 29.7 1 48 [HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels] 31 Figure 3.1b: Gantt chart for our SusAlgaeFuel Milestones (ML) and Deliverables (D) M2 M4 M6 M8 M10 M12 M14 M16 M18 M20 M22 M24 M26 M28 M30 M32 M34 M36 M38 M40 M42 M44 M46 M48 ML1.1, D1.1 ML D D1.2 D ML1.2 ML D1.3 D D.2.1 D D2.2 D D2.3 D ML3.1, D3.1 ML D ML3.2, D3.2 ML D ML4.1, D4.1 ML D ML4.2, D4.2 ML D ML4.3, D4.3 ML D ML4.4, D4.4 ML D ML4.5, D4.5 ML D ML5.1, D5.1 ML D D5.2 ML D ML6.1, ML6.2 ML ML ML6.3, ML6.4, D6.1, D6.2 ML D ML D ML6.5, D6.3 ML D D6.4 D D7.1, D7

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe communication activities, target audiences, key messages, and channels.
Write the Communication and open science section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.3   Investment readiness

Proposed measures to become investment ready and plans to commercialise the project outcomes: To become investment-ready and commercialise our project outcomes, we will implement a comprehensive strategy focused on business validation showing first traction (already Deceuninck, Veolia, PreZero, INEOS, Vynova are flagship references). Over the course of the project, we will secure engagement and traction with an additional 20 early adopters for cocreation, and we will secure LOIs from ≥3 early adopters interested in post-project pilots, sign 1 cooperation agreement with an OEM (e.g. PICVISA), and secure formal commitment or validation from ≥80% of our global network of distributors to include the VISUM LRS in their sales portfolio. In terms of our market positioning as a business, we already have a client base for other products in the VisumTM range, and we have been consolidating recognition and positioning with this brand in the deep tech space), and IP management (whereby IP will be safeguarded working with our IP legal experts ClarkeModet) to secure IPRs (patent, utility model, trademark) to protect our innovation and create licensing opportunities. We will develop a robust business and financing plan that includes a detailed market feasibility study, cost-benefit analysis, and financial projections to demonstrate the project’s commercial potential. Simultaneously, we will also validate and refine our Go-to-Market (GTM) strategy with the invaluable assistance of our international distribution network of channel partners.
Go-to-market pathway/strategy:
Our GTM strategy is to focus our initial market segment (market entry strategy) on plastics recycling, an industry worth >€9.1 billion in Europe, with 13.2 million tonnes of installed capacity across ⁓850 recycling facilities (source: Plastics Recyclers Europe). However, due to the impacts of cheap imported plastics, tough legislation and regulation of EU recycled plastics, high energy costs, among others, as well as by the end of 2025 the region will have lost recycling plants, amounting to a staggering 1 million tonnes of recycling capacity. The Plastics Recyclers Europe points to enhanced monitoring systems as being urgently needed.  VISUM LRS™ directly addresses this critical gap by enabling real-time, in-line detection of restricted substances and legacy additives, unlocking new value from difficult-to-recycle streams and helping recyclers meet quality standards more efficiently and cost-effectively. Our first use case in the PVC recycling segment is both strategic and pragmatic- strategic due to the strict regulations on lead content and other LS, and pragmatic because it was the target segment of the REMADYL project, through which we built relationships with lighthouse early adopters, including Deceuninck, Veolia, Ineos and PreZero. We also have received strong interest from a sorter OEM (PICVISA), enabling potential OEM partnerships for market scale-up.
By the end of the EIC Transition project, we expect to have brought the LIBS-Raman dual spectroscopy fusion system to TRL6. Full industrial deployment will require further steps in production scaling, integration testing, validation, and supply chain set up. Based on our market deployment experience, expected time-to-market (TTM) is 24-36 months post-project. This will also involve obtaining the necessary certifications for commercialisation, initially in the EU market (CE Mark, laser safety: DIRECTIVE 2006/25/EC, among others). We will engage early with regulatory bodies & experts (e.g. Asociación Española de Normalización (UNE), Deloitte for AI Act compliance) to fast-track certifications for building confidence and reducing customer adoption barriers.
In terms of Target Market Segmentation, as was seen from Table 1, within the waste recycling, there are 5 priority segments, and we will start with plastics recycling (sorting and identification), whereby in the PVC use case compliance with lead and other LS restrictions is critical. We will quickly expand to the adjacent and priority EU segments E-waste recycling and Battery recycling, and then to metals recycling & alloy sorting, and glass & construction waste streams. Our application pipeline will be planned for entry into other industrial applications in mining (which is a ‘spot’ implementation), pharmaceutical, food processing, and environmental monitoring sectors (in which the need for real-time hazardous material detection is growing).
The core Value Proposition will be refined highlighting the innovative dual spectroscopic fusion analyser's combination of elemental (LIBS) and molecular (Raman) analysis in one unit, providing real-time and accurate chemical composition analysis for complex and compliance critical applications (e.g. PVC recycling).
We will form early adopter partnerships with leading waste recycling companies (LOIs in place with Deceuninck, Veolia, PreZero + INEOS & Vynova as leading PVC producers), particularly those already struggling to meet new regulatory standards, as well as industry associations (e.g. SPIRE- LOI). The goal will be to sign ≥3 pilots or paid agreements (early-access pricing) for the post-project to establish market credibility and generate case studies showcasing the system’s effectiveness.
For our distribution and sales strategy, we will use a direct sales approach for early customers in the plastics recycling  industry, where the system’s technical benefits can be demonstrated effectively leveraging the PVC use case. Traction and experience with first clients will allow us to activate our global distribution network (covering 27 countries across Europe, Asia, Latin America, North Africa), who can scalably open international markets, brining local expertise and capacitated to offer robust technical support. In parallel, we will sign license agreements with OEMs (LOI already secured from PICVISA), who will integrate our inspection system in their sorters (new or retrofit opportunities). Both direct sales and our Channels will be supported by a digital marketing strategy, underpinned by paid campaigns and a professional content strategy for our website and social media channels to attract early adopters (lead generation). IRIS website will be continually optimised with SEO/SEM techniques to increase visibility and traction.
The business model is based on the provision of a turnkey implementations (one off revenue), in addition to annual service/maintenance agreements. A service-based model could also be explored, whereby the LIBS-Raman system is leased along with maintenance, technical support, and software updates. Our pricing strategy is based on cost + margin, with the annual maintenance service representing ⁓5% of the contract value (and is ARR). Financing options like leasing or pay-per-use models could be explored (for companies hesitant about upfront capital expenditure). Table 5 presents a 5-year business plan based on our GTM strategy (focussing first on PVC recycler, wider plastics recyclers and extending to adjacent segments within the online waste recycling market). This business plan will be further studied and updated in in WP3 & WP5. #§IMP-ACT-IA§#

3. Quality and efficiency of the implementation
